cues.label: A contract, not a step
cues.headline: The token ID must never move
cues.narration: A tokenizer is a contract, because the integer ID for a fragment must stay identical from training through inference.

cues.label: The bridge
cues.headline: Text becomes numbers
cues.narration: Models process numbers, so the tokenizer converts the whole corpus into integer sequences before training begins.

cues.label: Vocab 1000
cues.headline: A tiny vocabulary, a high UNK rate
cues.narration: With only a thousand tokens most subwords are missing, so real text collapses into UNK and its content is thrown away.

cues.label: Vocab 32000
cues.headline: LLaMA size, UNK drops away
cues.narration: At thirty two thousand tokens, the size LLaMA uses, most English is representable at about three to four characters per token.

cues.label: Vocab 262144
cues.headline: Gemma 4, UNK near zero
cues.narration: Gemma 4 uses two hundred sixty two thousand tokens, so tokens get longer and the UNK rate approaches zero even for technical text.

cues.label: BPE
cues.headline: Merge common pairs into subwords
cues.narration: BPE starts from characters and repeatedly merges the most common adjacent pair until it reaches the target vocabulary size.

cues.label: The shared contract
cues.headline: ID N always the same subword
cues.narration: The tokenizer and model share a contract where token ID N always maps to the same subword, from training through all inference.

cues.label: Swapping breaks it
cues.headline: A new tokenizer reads a new language
cues.narration: Swap the tokenizer and the IDs shift underneath the weights, so the model is suddenly reading a different language than it trained on.
